coredata.dc:title: Single-sized N-functionality graphene quantum dot in tunable dual-modality near infrared–I/II illumination detection and photodynamic therapy under multiphoton nonlinear excitation

Chang, Chan-Chi
coredata.dc:description: Doping sorted graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with heteroatoms and functionalizing them with amino acid could improve their radiative recombination and two-photon properties—including their excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence from the ultraviolet to the near-infrared-I (NIR-I) region, absorption, quantum yield, absolute cross section, lifetime, and radiative-to-nonradiative decay ratio—under two-photon excitation (TPE) at a low excitation energy and short photoexcitation duration, as determined using a self-made optical microscopy system with a femtosecond Ti–sapphire laser. Four types of sorted GQDs were investigated: undoped GQDs, nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), amino-functionalized GQDs (amino-GQDs), and N-doped and amino-functionalized GQDs (amino-N-GQDs). Among them, the sorted amino-N-GQDs are effective as a two-photon photosensitizer and generate the highest quantity of reactive oxygen species for the elimination of multidrug-resistant cancer cells through two-photon photodynamic therapy (PDT). Larger amino-N-GQDs result in a greater number of C–N and N–functionalities, leading to a superior photochemical effect and more favorable intrinsic luminescence properties, making the dots effective contrast agents for tracking and localizing cancer cells during in-depth bioimaging in a three-dimensional biological environment under TPE in the NIR-II region. Overall, this study highlights the potential of large amino-N-GQDs as a material for future application to dual-modality two-photon PDT and biomedical imaging.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optical setup and the femtosecond laser optical imaging system.
Conceptual schematic of the GQD-based materials: (a) GQD, (b) N-GQD, (c) amino-GQD and (d) amino-N-GQD, respectively.

HR-TEM images of (a) GQD0.7, (b) GQD4.8, and (c) GQD12.2; (d) N-GQD0.8, (e) N-GQD4.9, and (f) N-GQD12.2; (g) amino-GQD0.8, (h) amino-GQD5.0, and (i) amino-GQD12.3; and (j) amino-N-GQD0.9, (k) amino-N-GQD5.0, and (l) amino-N-GQD12.3. The images depict graphene {1
1 −
00} lattice planes with 0.213-nm d-spacing. The sorted materials exhibited different PL emissions at wavelengths encompassing the (a,d,g,j insets) UV, (b,e,h,k insets) visible, to (c,f,i,l insets) NIR regions.

(a) Z-axis scan of a thin gold film used for measuring the second harmonic generation signal at various positions. The laser system's z-axis resolution (full width at half maximum) was 0.79, 1.01, and 1.17 μm (fit using the Gaussian function) for excitation wavelengths of 820, 890, and 980 nm, respectively. Relative TPA spectra of (b) smallest, (c) middle-size, and (d) largest sorted GQD-based materials. TPE was executed as a function of wavelength (800–1000 nm) at approximately 160.0 nJ pixel−1 to monitor signals. Data are means ± SD (n = 6). PL intensity versus excitation power (logarithm) of (e) smallest, (f) middle-size, and (g) largest sorted GQD-based materials under TPE at power levels ranging from 1278 to 4793 nJ pixel−1 (Ex: 820 nm), 1230–4613 nJ pixel−1 (Ex: 890 nm), and 1299–4873 nJ pixel−1 (Ex: 980 nm), respectively. R 2 > 0.999. All of the calculated energy values in these figures pertain to the photoexcited power after the objective (on the sample) throughout the text.

(a–d) Relative TPL spectra of materials subjected to TPE (power: 160.0 nJ pixel−1). (e–h) Photostability of materials and fluorophores subjected to TPE (power: 2274 nJ pixel−1) over 50,000 scans (for the smallest, middle-size, and largest sorted GQD-based materials, total effective exposure time was 238, 280.5, and 340 s under excitation at a wavelength of 820, 890, and 980 nm, respectively). Data are means ± SD (n = 6). (i–l) Time-resolved room-temperature PL decay profiles of materials (TPE power: 160.0 nJ pixel−1; excitation wavelength of 820, 890, and 980 nm for the smallest, middle-size, and largest sorted GQD-based materials, respectively). The measured values of (a–d) and (e–h) were shown in Tables S1 and S2, respectively. Delivered dose a solution of material: 0.4 μg mL−1.

(a–d) Uptake assay using the sorted dot−AbEGFR-treated KB-50 and sorted dot−treated KB-50. Data are the means ± SD (n = 6). (e–h) Phosphorescence spectra of the sorted dots at 1270 nm. Delivered dose a solution of material: 0.4 μg mL−1.

(a) Quantified viability (%) of KB-50 cells treated with the mixture of amino-N-GQD5.0–Abmicrotubule and amino-N-GQD12.3–AbEGFR and subjected to TPE (power: 160.0 nJ pixel−1, with 150 scans, corresponding to a total effective exposure time of 1.02 s under excitation at a wavelength of 980 nm; ****p < 0.0001 through Student's t-test). Delivered dose a solution of material: 0.8 μg mL−1. Data are means ± SD (n = 6). TPI of KB-50 cancer cells alone was performed at a depth of 260 μm (b) without or (c) with laser irradiation; (d) cells treated with the mixture of dots–Abs without irradiation; (e) cells treated with the mixture of dots–Abs and (f) lacking of antibody conjugations and subjected to TPE (amino-N-GQD5.0 and amino-N-GQD12.3 emissions in green and gray). (g) TPAF image (gray level) of unlabeled cells captured at the same depth and TPE power or (h) a higher power of 2274 nJ pixel−1 with 20,000 scans (corresponding to a total effective exposure time of 136 s under excitation at a wavelength of 980 nm). Images of the (i) cells treated with the mixture of dots–Abs and (j) unlabeled cells under one-photon excitation with a 633-nm laser (0.18 W cm−2) at the same depth. Nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue). Scale bar: 30 μm.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doping sorted graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with heteroatoms and functionalizing them with amino acid could improve their radiative recombination and two-photon properties—including their excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence from the ultraviolet to the near-infrared-I (NIR-I) region, absorption, quantum yield, absolute cross section, lifetime, and radiative-to-nonradiative decay ratio—under two-photon excitation (TPE) at a low excitation energy and short photoexcitation duration, as determined using a self-made optical microscopy system with a femtosecond Ti–sapphire laser. Four types of sorted GQDs were investigated: undoped GQDs, nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), amino-functionalized GQDs (amino-GQDs), and N-doped and amino-functionalized GQDs (amino-N-GQDs). Among them, the sorted amino-N-GQDs are effective as a two-photon photosensitizer and generate the highest quantity of reactive oxygen species for the elimination of multidrug-resistant cancer cells through two-photon photodynamic therapy (PDT). Larger amino-N-GQDs result in a greater number of C–N and N–functionalities, leading to a superior photochemical effect and more favorable intrinsic luminescence properties, making the dots effective contrast agents for tracking and localizing cancer cells during in-depth bioimaging in a three-dimensional biological environment under TPE in the NIR-II region. Overall, this study highlights the potential of large amino-N-GQDs as a material for future application to dual-modality two-photon PDT and biomedical imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained considerable attention in various fields owing to their unique properties. One of the advantages of GQDs is that they are easy to fabricate, inexpensive, and nontoxic, rendering them an attractive alternative to other materials (Torres, 2017). Additionally, GQDs have a large specific surface area, which enables strong interaction with other materials; they also exhibit unique electron-transfer properties, which render them useful in electronic and optoelectronic devices. GQDs can also be easily functionalized; therefore, their surface properties can be modified to tailor their properties to specific applications (Kurzątkowska et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2016; Stobiecka et al., 2016; Torres, 2017). In particular, surface groups on or the functionalization of GQDs can substantially affect the dots' optical and electronic properties, including their photoluminescence (PL) mechanism. GQDs' intrinsic-state emissions can result from quantum confinement effects that are engendered by the small size of the dots. Such confinement effects can lead to discrete energy levels and cause transitions between these levels, resulting in characteristic emission spectra (Gong et al., 2019). Furthermore, the zigzag edge sites of GQDs possess unique electronic properties that can contribute to intrinsic-state emissions. However, defects or impurities on the surface of the dots can engender energy traps and cause defect-state emissions. Such energy traps can trap excited electrons and holes and induce their recombination, producing other characteristic emission spectra (Sun et al., 2020). As mentioned, surface groups on or functionalization of GQDs can affect the dots' PL mechanism by altering the energy levels and defects on the dots' surface. Accordingly, modifying such surface groups enables the control of the emission properties of GQDs, such as their emission wavelength, quantum yield (QY), and lifetime (Gong et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020). The PL emission of GQDs determines their suitability for photochemistry, bioimaging, and biomedical research (Lee et al., 2016). However, elucidating the quantum-confinement sp2 domain size effect of GQDs can be challenging because of the existence of zigzag edge sites and surface defect states. The zigzag edge sites of GQDs can lead to size-dependent and excitation-wavelength-dependent (EWD) PL, which can complicate the interpretation of experimental results. Moreover, surface defect states can contribute to size-dependent and EWD-PL because such states can act as energy traps or recombination centers (Li et al., 2021; Siddique et al., 2021). This can result in changes in the emission properties of GQDs as a function of the dots' size and excitation wavelength. The EWD-PL phenomenon exhibited by GQDs is often attributed to the existence of inhomogeneous oxygen functionalities on the dots' surface (Kumar et al., 2020). These O functionalities can act as energy traps. However, the aforementioned inhomogeneity means that GQD-based materials are not necessarily suitable for use as probes in optical applications such as bioimaging, in which the emission properties must be precisely controlled and reproducible. The existence of inhomogeneous O functionalities can result in a broad emission spectrum and reduced QY, which can limit the effectiveness of GQDs in the aforementioned applications (Wang et al., 2014). Such inhomogeneity can be addressed through the use of sorted GQDs. Sorted-GQD-based materials with a homogeneous distribution of O or heteroatom dopant functionalities can be obtained by sieving the materials through membranes with pores of various sizes, a technique known as size-selective sieving. This technique can yield GQD samples with a relatively narrow size distribution and uniform surface chemistry. In addition, this technique can facilitate the recombination of photogenerated charges, which can result in the fabricated material exhibiting excitation-wavelength-independent (EWI) PL emissions. EWI-PL emissions enable a clearer interpretation of optical-property-related intrinsic electronic behaviors that are attributable to quantum confinement effects. Additionally, size-selective sieving enables the fabrication of GQD-based materials with tunable photochemical properties. The properties of GQD-based materials can be tailored to specific applications, including phototherapy or bioimaging, by controlling the materials’ size and surface chemistry.

The chemical composition of sorted materials such as GQDs can be modified through doping, which can confer such materials with altered electrocatalytic, electrochemical, or photochemical activities. N doping is a common method for modifying the electronic and optical properties of GQDs; the N dopants act as electron-accepting atoms, and because their atomic size is comparable to that of carbon (C) atoms, they can be incorporated into the C lattice of the material without substantially disrupting the crystal structure of the material. N dopants can impart a relatively high positive charge density to adjacent C atoms in the GQD lattice, which can alter the electronic properties of the material; this alteration can in turn engender changes in the electrocatalytic, electrochemical, or photochemical activities of the material. Furthermore, N doping can remove vacancy defects in the C lattice of GQDs—improving their overall quality and performance (Moon et al., 2021)—and can create n-type domains in the material, enhancing the electrical conductivity of the GQDs and hence making them more effective for use in electronic devices. For GQDs exhibiting remarkable quantum confinement and edge effects, doping with bonded N atoms is highly useful for achieving photodynamic action and tunable luminescence in bioimaging by modifying the intrinsic properties of the GQDs. The doped heteroatom has strong effects on the carrier density, chemical composition, and band gap of GQDs; thus, doping results in optical, electrical, and photochemical properties that are completely distinct from those of nondoped GQDs (Trauzettel et al., 2007). Additionally, modifying the chemical composition of GQDs by using primary amine molecules (amino group functionalization) can considerably alter the electronic properties of GQD-based materials. Such modification introduces strong electron-donating groups, which contribute to the effect on the electronic properties of GQDs-based materials (Wang et al., 2009). This effect is particularly evident when GQDs are functionalized by amino acids; the resultant GQDs can exhibit singlet–triplet splitting and efficient intersystem crossing (Li and Yan, 2010). This process compensates for the internal conversion between multiplicity-identical states, resulting in enhanced photochemical and electrochemical activities (Zhao et al., 2017). The electro-optical properties of amino-acid-functionalized GQDs are distinct from those of nonfunctionalized GQDs, providing advantages for biomedical and optoelectronic applications. Accordingly, amino group functionalization is a promising strategy for the design and optimization of GQD-based materials with properties tailored for specific applications (Jiang et al., 2019).

Several studies have investigated the use of multiplexed GQD-based materials combined with multiphoton excitation as contrast probes (Gong et al., 2019; Moon et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2020; Torres, 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). Multiphoton microscopy is a laser scanning technique in which fluorescence is induced through localized nonlinear excitation; this technique is thus an attractive approach for photochemical applications. Two-photon excitation (TPE) laser microscopy, a multiphoton microscopy approach, is a type of laser scanning microscopy used in biological imaging. It entails the use of near-infrared (NIR) laser excitation, typically in the NIR-I or NIR-II range, to deeply penetrate biological tissues with minimal scattering and absorption, which could not be achieved with visible light (B. Li et al., 2020). Accordingly, TPE enables deep imaging of tissues without the risk of damaging or affecting the biological sample. Moreover, the use of multiphoton microscopy with NIR-I/NIR-II excitation reduces the magnitude of photobleaching, which is the loss of fluorescence intensity due to repeated excitation of a fluorophore (Gong et al., 2019; Torres, 2017). This enables long-term imaging of biological samples without causing damage to the specimen. Multiphoton microscopy also requires less energy than do other types of microscopy, and this can help reduce the risk of photodamage to biological samples (Moon et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2020). However, to fully realize the potential of this technique, highly photostable and biocompatible materials, such as GQD-based materials, must be developed. Such development is a crucial step toward the use of two-photon microscopy and other multiphoton techniques in biomedical research and clinical applications.

This paper describes the preparation of four types of GQD-based materials—undoped GQDs, bonded-N-atom-doped GQDs (N-GQDs), amino-acid-functionalized GQDs (amino-GQDs), and N-doped amino-acid-functionalized GQDs (amino-N-GQDs)—which were obtained by subjecting graphene oxide sheets to an ultrasonic shearing reaction in a modification of Hummers' method (Hummers and Offeman, 1958). A self-made inverted optical microscopy system with a femtosecond titanium–sapphire laser (repetition rate = 80 MHz; Mai Tai laser with optical parametric oscillators; Spectra-Physics, USA; Fig. 1 ) was employed to conduct two-photon studies on the prepared GQD-based materials. This system enabled visualization and analysis of the materials at high spatial and temporal resolutions, rendering it a useful tool for studying the properties and behavior of GQD-based materials. The results revealed that sieving the GQD-based materials through polypropylene membranes yielded materials with homogeneous O and N distributions, high crystallinity, and high uniformity. When subjected to TPE, these materials exhibited EWI two-photon luminescence (TPL) from the ultraviolet (UV) to the NIR region. This finding is notable because it suggests that these GQD-based materials have potential applications in various fields for which EWI-TPL emissions can be advantageous, such as bioimaging. The use of the GQD-based materials obtained in this study may also facilitate greater reproducibility and consistency. Additionally, when the mean lateral size of the sorted GQDs was larger, the dots' two-photon properties—such as two-photon absorption (TPA), TPL, two-photon absolute cross section, two-photon radiative-to-nonradiative decay ratio, two-photon stability, lifetime, and QY—were improved, and the GQDs generated a relatively high quantity of reactive oxygen species (ROS) during two-photon photodynamic therapy (PDT). This increased ROS generation enhanced the efficiency of eliminating a multidrug-resistant strain of cancer cells under weak TPE and a short photoexcitation time. Furthermore, the study explored the two-photon properties of the sorted GQD-based materials by using TPE microscopy in the NIR spectrum. The results revealed that the sorted N-GQDs and amino-GQDs exhibited superior two-photon properties to the sorted GQDs without N doping or amino acid functionalization. The N dopant in the sorted N-GQDs was discovered to play a critical role in the efficient elimination of multidrug-resistant species through two-photon PDT; the amino group functionalization was also observed to play a crucial role in the elimination efficiency of the GQD-based materials. Such functionalization also resulted in the enhancement of the sorted dots’ effectiveness as contrast probes. The sorted amino-N-GQDs had a detectable quantum confinement effect and high PL QY (Trauzettel et al., 2007). Strong electron donation and a large π-conjugated system in N-doped and amino-group-functionalized GQD materials (Kumar et al., 2022) can lead to remarkable two-photon properties. These properties make these materials promising as two-photon contrast probes for biomedical imaging applications, and the materials can have altered electronic properties that result in high charge transfer efficiency (Tetsuka et al., 2012). The two-photon properties that are achievable include high QY and large TPE cross section. Materials exhibiting these properties can serve as effective contrast agents in two-photon imaging (TPI). Overall, the electronic properties of N-doped and amino-group-functionalized GQD materials are a critical factor affecting their suitability as two-photon contrast probes. The unique properties of these materials make them a promising area of exploration for research into TPI, two-photon phototherapy, and other biomedical applications. In summary, the findings of this study provide insights into the optical and electronic properties of GQD-based materials and demonstrate the potential applications of these materials in various fields such as electronics and biomedicine.

For effective photochemical and biomedical examinations, a scanning microscopy system coupled with a nonlinear multiphoton femtosecond laser should be used because such a system offers the advantage of excitation wavelengths in the NIR-I and NIR-II windows and thus a higher resolution along the z–axis than along the x–y axis. Specifically, the focal spot resolution along the z–axis is 0.79 μm at an excitation wavelength of 820 nm (Fig. 4 a), whereas that along the x–y axis is 0.38 μm (Fig. 1). The relationship between focal spot size and excitation wavelength can be theoretically understood using the diffraction equation, which suggests that a longer excitation wavelength results in a larger focal spot size after the optical objective. This relationship is supported by the numerical values obtained for the x–y axis and z–axis focal spot resolutions for TPE at excitation wavelengths of 890 nm (∼0.42 and 1.01 μm, respectively) and 980 nm (∼0.46 and 1.17 μm, respectively; please see the Supplementary Information for details regarding the calculations). These values suggest that the system can obtain high-resolution images, particularly along the z–axis, which is important for imaging deep within a sample. The system also has several advantages such as excitation wavelengths in the NIR-I and NIR-II windows, which also enable deeper imaging; low average laser power, which helps reduce the magnitude of photodamage; and long-term deep-exploration capability. This study observed that the most efficient excitation wavelengths for the smallest, middle-size, and largest sorted GQD-based materials (i.e., those of size 0.7–0.9, 4.8–5.0, and 12.2–12.3 nm) were 820, 890, and 980 nm, respectively (Fig. 4b–d). The reason for this high efficiency was likely the involvement of interband transitions, which are also known as TPA. This phenomenon involves the absorption of two photons of lower energy to excite an electron to a higher energy state and can lead to efficient excitation of the material at lower energy levels (Lesani et al., 2021). Applying the most efficient TPE wavelengths can substantially improve the two-photon properties for photochemical and biomedical studies. The TPL spectra of the various GQDs fabricated in this study (Fig. 4e–g) and those of fluorophores such as rhodamine B and fluorescein (Fig. S16) were obtained and assessed. The assessment results indicated the existence of a two-photon process that was quadratically dependent on the TPE power. This means that as the TPE power was increased (logarithmically), the frequency of the two-photon process increased in a quadratic (or squared) manner. The corresponding exponent for this relationship was found to be 2.00 ± 0.03, which suggests a strong correlation between the TPE power and the frequency of the two-photon process. The peaks in the TPL spectra of the GQDs with various lateral sizes exhibited a red shift, signifying that the larger GQDs had a smaller energy gap and that less energy was required to excite electrons to a higher energy level. Specifically, the peaks in the TPL spectra of GQD0.7, GQD4.8, and GQD12.2 exhibited a red shift at approximately 372, 545, and 849 nm, respectively (Fig. 5 a). A similar trend was found for the N-GQDs: the peaks in the TPL spectra of N-GQD0.8, N-GQD4.9, and N-GQD12.2 exhibited a red shift at approximately 375, 552, and 853 nm, respectively (Fig. 5b). The amino group functionalization of the GQDs also affected their PL properties. The peaks in the TPL spectra of the amino-functionalized GQDs exhibited a red shift compared with those in the spectra of the nonfunctionalized GQDs. Specifically, the peaks in the TPL spectra of amino-GQD0.8, amino-GQD5.0, and amino-GQD12.3 exhibited a red shift at approximately 376, 552, and 858 nm, respectively (Fig. 5c). Similarly, the peaks in the TPL spectra of the amino-N-GQDs exhibited a red shift compared with those in the spectra of the nonfunctionalized N-GQDs; the peaks in the spectra of amino-N-GQD0.9, amino-N-GQD5.0, and amino-N-GQD12.3 exhibited a red shift at approximately 379, 559, and 864 nm, respectively (Fig. 5d). All of the TPE-associated TPL peaks followed a similar trend to those observed for the emission wavelength derived through one-photon excitation; that is, the sorted GQD-based materials exhibited EWI-PL emissions (Fig. S12). This indicates that the emission wavelength was independent of the excitation wavelength, which is desirable for practical applications. Another feature of the sorted GQD-based materials was their high stability. After the materials had been stored for a period of 3 months after their fabrication, their stability was evaluated by observing their TPL intensity following TPE. The results revealed almost no decrease in the TPL intensity of the as-prepared sorted GQD-based materials when they were subjected to 50,000 scans of TPE at a power of approximately 2274 nJ pixel−1 (for the smallest, middle-size, and largest sorted GQD-based materials, this corresponded to total effective exposure times of 238, 280.5, and 340 s under excitation at wavelengths of 820, 890, and 980 nm, respectively; please see the Supplementary Information for details regarding the calculations; Fig. 5e–h). The materials thus exhibited a high level of photostability, which means they were resistant to degradation and fading when exposed to light. This photostability was retained even after 3 months of storage and 50,000 scans involving light exposure. The materials were found to be considerably superior to conventional fluorophores such as rhodamine B and fluorescein, which are known to undergo photobleaching or gradually begin to emit less intense fluorescence when exposed to light. The post-TPE TPL intensity of the dots, which is a measure of their fluorescence intensity, was found to be higher in the case of the new materials, leading to less photobleaching. Therefore, these results suggest that these materials could be a promising alternative to conventional fluorophores in applications that require high photostability and that they can serve as an efficient tool for two-photon investigations.

The sorted GQD-based materials had a large TPE cross section, indicating that they had a high probability of undergoing TPE (Zipfel et al., 2003). This was likely due to the presence of intrinsic fluorophores in the materials, which have high QY and are thus efficient at converting absorbed photons into emitted photons. Consequently, when the materials were measured under TPE, they generated strong TPL signals. Compared with that of a reference material [rhodamine B (Xu and Webb, 1996); please see the Supplementary Information for details regarding the calculations, Tables S3–S4], the absolute cross sections of GQD0.7, GQD4.8, and GQD12.2 ranged from 41,838 to 52,514 GM (Goeppert–Mayer units, with 1 GM = 10−50 cm4 s photon−1; Table S5), those of N-GQD0.8, N-GQD4.9, and N-GQD12.2 ranged from 44,570 to 58,626 GM, those of amino-GQD0.8, amino-GQD5.0, and amino-GQD12.3 ranged from 45,198 to 60,198 GM, and those of amino-N-GQD0.9, amino-N-GQD5.0, and amino-N-GQD12.3 ranged from 46,561 to 62,357 GM. These values were noted to be considerably higher (at least 280 times higher) than those of conventional fluorophores [e.g., rhodamine B and fluorescein: 157.0 and 29.9 GM (Ex: 820 nm); 20.9 and 13.6 GM (Ex: 890 nm); and 19.4 and 16.2 GM (Ex: 980 nm); Table S3]. This major difference in magnitude confirmed the high ratio of energy absorbed to the input-energy flux in biospecimens, which is favorable for highly effective TPE-associated investigations. This property makes the materials potentially useful for a range of applications, including imaging and sensing in biological systems. This study also determined that the absolute fluorescence QYs (Würth et al., 2013) of GQD0.7, GQD4.8, and GQD12.2 were 0.17, 0.25, and 0.39, respectively; those of N-GQD0.8, N-GQD4.9, and N-GQD12.2 were 0.19, 0.28, and 0.41, respectively; those of amino-GQD0.8, amino-GQD5.0, and amino-GQD12.3 were 0.20, 0.30, and 0.42, respectively; and those of amino-N-GQD0.9, amino-N-GQD5.0, and amino-N-GQD12.3 were 0.23, 0.35, and 0.46, respectively (Table S5). The estimated fluorescence QY values were consistent with the XPS results (Figs. S7–S10), and this is because the existence of certain functional groups—such as C–O, C=O, O=C–O, and amide–N groups—can lead to the nonradiative recombination of local electron–hole pairs and prevent intrinsic-state emissions in small materials (Yuan et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2012). The average lifetime of the sorted GQD-based materials was determined using a time-correlated single-photon-counting technique involving a triple-exponential fitting function. The average lifetime of GQD0.7 was found to be 1.61 ns; this value was derived from the observed lifetimes of 0.23, 1.21, and 6.54 ns (as detailed in Fig. 5i–l, and Tables S5–S6). Moreover, the average lifetimes of GQD4.8 and GQD12.2 were 1.58 and 1.35 ns, respectively; those of N-GQD0.8, N-GQD4.9, and N-GQD12.2 ranged from 1.44 to 1.22 ns; those of amino-GQD0.8, amino-GQD5.0, and amino-GQD12.3 ranged from 1.33 to 1.12 ns; and those of amino-N-GQD0.9, amino-N-GQD5.0, and amino-N-GQD12.3 ranged from 1.27 to 1.01 ns. The sequential radiative-to-nonradiative decay ratio is a measure of the efficiency of radiative decay relative to that of nonradiative decay in a material. In this study, the sequential radiative-to-nonradiative decay ratio was calculated as approximately 0.21 for GQD0.7, 0.33 for GQD4.8, and 0.64 for GQD12.2. Moreover, the ratio was approximately 0.23 for N-GQD0.8, 0.39 for N-GQD4.9, and 0.69 for N-GQD12.2. The ratio was also approximately 0.25 for amino-GQD0.8, 0.43 for amino-GQD5.0, and 0.73 for amino-GQD12.3. Finally, the ratio was approximately 0.30 for amino-N-GQD0.9, 0.54 for amino-N-GQD5.0, and 0.85 for amino-N-GQD12.3 (Table S5). These ratio values indicated that radiative decay was more efficient than was nonradiative decay in the materials, and this property is favorable in terms of their use as efficient and sensitive fluorescent probes in biological imaging and sensing applications. The findings of this study suggest that shortening the lifetime and increasing the QY can favor the radiative pathway over the nonradiative pathway for materials subjected to TPE. The restoration of sp2 hybridization in GQDs with bonded N atoms can result in delocalized electrons entering π* states and donating electrons, which can increase the frequency of radiative electron–hole pair recombination that is induced by N dopants and amino groups on the surface of the sorted GQD-based materials, leading to an increase in intrinsic state emission. However, the presence of edge amine groups on N-doped and amino-functionalized GQDs can raise the maximum occupied molecular orbital energy of graphene flakes (Kumar et al., 2022). This narrowing of the orbital band gap can increase the PL QY because resonance occurs between the delocalized π orbitals and the molecular orbitals of the primary amine groups (Wang et al., 2009), which in turn increases the absolute TPE cross section. Moreover, increasing the mean lateral size of sorted GQD-based materials can lead to major improvements in their PL QY and two-photon properties, making the materials more efficient for various applications. This is because larger GQDs tend to have more numerous delocalized electrons, which can enhance the dots' absorption and emission properties. Additionally, the greater surface area offered by larger GQDs provides more numerous reactive sites for chemical functionalization, which can further improve the dots’ electronic and optical properties.

Multidrug resistance (MDR), a major obstacle to the effective treatment of cancer, is characterized by the ability of cancer cells to become resistant to various chemotherapy drugs. This resistance can develop through several mechanisms, one of which is an increase in cancer cells' capacity to repair their DNA. Chemotherapy drugs work by damaging the DNA of cancer cells, which triggers cell apoptosis (cell death). However, cancer cells with increased DNA repair capacity can more effectively repair the drug-induced damage, reducing the effectiveness of chemotherapy. Another mechanism is dysfunction in the apoptosis pathway. Apoptosis is a natural process through which cells are programmed to die, and it is triggered when a cell is damaged or no longer needed. Cancer cells with dysfunction in this pathway may be less likely to undergo apoptosis in response to chemotherapy, and this effect can contribute to drug resistance (Vasan et al., 2019). Combination therapy and nanotechnology-based techniques are promising approaches for overcoming multidrug resistance. Nanoparticle-based therapy systems have considerable potential in terms of enhancing the efficacy of drugs against MDR species. Many PSs are substrates of efflux transporters, and this limits the efficacy of PDT. Accordingly, an active nanoplatform can provide a means of reducing MDR in cancer cells and improve the efficacy of PDT. To explore the potential of the sorted GQD-based materials as two-photon PSs, human cervical carcinoma cells (KB-50) that were developed to be resistant to paclitaxel were employed as a multidrug-resistant analyte in this study. To enhance specificity and accuracy in the determination of two-photon PDT efficiency, the surfaces of the sorted dots were conjugated with a specific epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) antibody (AbEGFR) in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions (Conjugation Kit, abcam, Cambridge, UK; Modification kit, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) (Ning et al., 2008; Rizvi et al., 2017). Uptake assay results (Fig. 6 a–d) revealed that the biocompatible dots (Fig. S17) that were conjugated with the antibody had higher uptake efficiency than did those without antibody conjugation. High uptake efficiency was observed in the first 6 h of incubation (82%–83%, 80%–82%, and 79%–81% for the smallest, middle-size, and largest sorted GQD-based materials, respectively), and the efficiency plateaued after 6 h (83%–85% for all sorted dots). By contrast, the dots without antibody conjugation had relatively low uptake efficiency in the first 6 h (42%–45%, 38%–42%, and 33%–39% for the smallest, middle-size, and largest sorted GQD-based materials, respectively), and the efficiency plateaued after 24 h of incubation (80%–82% for all sorted dots). The relatively high uptake efficiency and higher rate of uptake discovered for the AbEGFR-conjugated sorted dots can be attributed to the successful conjugation, which gave the dots highly specific binding ability that facilitated the uptake of a substantial quantity of the sorted dots into the cells. These results suggest that conjugation with specific antibodies can enhance the uptake efficiency of sorted dots, and this finding may have valuable implications for the use of sorted GQDs in various applications such as drug delivery and imaging.

Oxidative stress generated by ROS such as singlet oxygen (1O2), the hydroxyl radical (OH . ), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂), and the superoxide anion radical (O2
.− ) can induce various processes and reactions that contribute to damage to the biological substrates surrounding photoexcited-material-treated tumor cells. This ultimately results in the failure of normal functioning in redox reactions and the death or irreparable damage of tumor cells (Kuo et al., 2017). Accordingly, ROS are considered to play a crucial role in PDT. In the present study, the sorted GQD-based materials were directly employed as photosensitizers (PSs) to generate ROS. Two-photon PDT was then performed by simultaneously using a combination of excited triplet-state materials, oxygen and light configured to a suitable wavelength and energy; and singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG), trans-1-(2′-methoxyvinyl)pyrene (t-MVP), 2,3-bis (2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide (XTT), γ-L-glutamyl-L-cysteinyl-glycine (glutathione, GSH), and 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA) reagents. Signals generated by the sorted dots after TPE (power: 160.0 nJ pixel−1) and corresponding to 1O2, OH . , H2O2, and O2
.− were detected to determine ROS action (Tables S7–S18). Overall, for the smallest materials, 100, 125, and 150 scans were performed, corresponding to total effective exposure times of approximately 0.48, 0.60, and 0.71 s, respectively, under excitation at a wavelength of 820 nm; for the middle-size materials, 100, 125, and 150 scans were performed, corresponding to total effective exposure times of approximately 0.56, 0.70, and 0.84 s, respectively, under excitation at a wavelength of 890 nm; and for the largest materials, 100, 125, and 150 scans were performed, corresponding to total effective exposure times of approximately 0.68, 0.85, and 1.02 s, respectively, under excitation at a wavelength of 980 nm (please see the Supplementary Information for details regarding the calculations). To prevent any inadvertent production of ROS due to exposure to white light (Kuo et al., 2017), the PDT-related experiments were conducted in the dark. The aim of the experiments was to investigate the generation of ROS for sorted GQD-based materials of various sizes and compositions. The results indicated that when the dots were larger, they generated a larger amount of ROS. Furthermore, of the four types of GQDs investigated, the N-doped and amino-functionalized GQDs induced the most abundant ROS. This suggests that N doping and amino group functionalization of GQDs may enhance their ability to generate ROS, and this property could have applications in areas such as biomedical imaging and phototherapy.

Prior to performing two-photon PDT, this study determined the ROS generated by malignant KB-50 cells treated with the sorted dots (namely the sorted dot−AbEGFR-treated KB-50; Tables S11–S14). The other sorted dots without antibody conjugation were used for comparison (Tables S15–S18). The sorted dots were discovered to have notable ability to produce ROS through TPE, which suggests their potential for use in PDT. Notably, the sorted amino-N-GQDs exhibited higher ROS creation ability than did the sorted dots free of amino functionalization and N doping (i.e., the sorted GQDs), the sorted N-GQDs, and the sorted amino-GQDs. This suggests that the existence of amino–N groups on the sorted dots enhanced the dots’ ability to produce ROS, and this ability is crucial for their potential use in PDT. This finding is consistent with those presented in Tables S7–S10. The sorted dots without antibody conjugation produced less ROS than did those with antibody conjugation, demonstrating that the successful conjugation of the materials with the antibody yielded high efficiency, specificity, and selectivity. Similarly, Fig. 6e–h presents the signals of 1O2 phosphorescence emitted from the sorted dots at 1270 nm, demonstrating that greater dot size resulted in higher intensity, with the trend similar to that detailed in Tables S7–S18. Specifically, when the surface area of the sorted dots was greater, the degree of elimination of cancer cells was also greater, along with the amount of ROS generated. These results signify that the sorted dots have potential for use in PDT owing to their ability to generate ROS, which can be used to kill cancer cells. This study estimated the 1O2 QY (ΦΔ) values of the various types of sorted dots; overall, the ΦΔ values ranged from 0.20 to 0.34. Specifically, the ΦΔ values were approximately 0.20, 0.24, and 0.27 for GQD0.7, GQD4.8, and GQD12.2, respectively; approximately 0.22, 0.25, and 0.30 for N-GQD0.8, N-GQD4.9, and N-GQD12.2, respectively; approximately 0.23, 0.27, and 0.31 for amino-GQD0.8, amino-GQD5.0, and amino-GQD12.3, respectively; and approximately 0.26, 0.30, and 0.34 for amino-N-GQD0.9, amino-N-GQD5.0, and amino-N-GQD12.3, respectively [for reference, ΦΔ = 0.64 for meso-tetra(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine dihydrochloride dissolved in D2O (Shi et al., 2006)]. Higher values indicate greater efficiency in generating 1O2, a key component in PDT. Because the sorted amino-N-GQDs exhibited a favorable two-photon absolute cross section, PL QY, lifetime, two-photon radiative-to-nonradiative decay ratio, ΦΔ, and ROS generation ability, this study used them in subsequent experiments.

Experiments were conducted to investigate the potential ability of the sorted amino-N-GQDs to eliminate MDR strains of malignant KB-50 cells through PDT. The elimination capability of the sorted amino-N-GQDs was evaluated in the dark by using a low dose of dots (0.40 μg mL−1). TPE was performed at a power of approximately 160.0 nJ pixel−1 (with 150 scans for sorted amino-N-GQD0.9–12.3, corresponding to total effective exposure times of 0.71, 0.84, and 1.02 s under excitation at wavelengths of 820, 890, and 980 nm, respectively; please see the Supplementary Information for details regarding the calculations). Regardless of whether laser exposure was applied, the amino-N-GQDs without antibody conjugation did not eliminate KB-50 cells (Fig. S18); that is, they did not reduce the cells’ viability. By contrast, when photoexcitation was applied, treatment with amino-N-GQD0.9–AbEGFR resulted in a KB-50 cell viability of approximately 17%; additionally, treatment with amino-N-GQD5.0–AbEGFR (i.e., the complex containing middle-size dots conjugated with the antibody) further reduced KB-50 cell viability (approximately 2%). The KB-50 cells were completely eliminated when they were treated with amino-N-GQD12.3–AbEGFR.

Prior to TPI, this study investigated the KB-50 cell elimination ability of amino-N-GQD5.0 and amino-N-GQD12.3 conjugated with an anti-microtubule antibody and an antinuclear and mitotic cell antigen antibody [amino-N-GQD5.0–Abmicrotubule and amino-N-GQD12.3–Abnucleus, respectively; prepared in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions (conjugation Kit, abcam, Cambridge, UK; modification kit, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA (Ning et al., 2008; Rizvi et al., 2017)] under TPE. Under the same treatment, the mixture of dot–Abmicrotubule and dot–Abnucleus materials exhibited excellent efficiency (100%) in eliminating KB-50 cells; this efficiency was higher than that of the dots without antibody conjugation (Fig. 7 a). The amino-N-GQDs’ favorable absorption, QY, luminescence, absolute cross section, stability, lifetime, radiative-to-nonradiative decay ratio under TPE, and high photostability (Fig. 5e–h and Table S3) suggest that they may be effective contrast agents for the imaging of deep biological tissue to obtain clear images. Additionally, the use of TPE at NIR-II wavelengths may enable light to penetrate deeper tissue, enabling the imaging of structures deep within the collagen matrix to mimic a three-dimensional biological environment (C. Li et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2020). Accordingly, this study conducted a bioimaging test, and Fig. 7b–j presents the bioimaging test results. Nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue). The PL emitted from KB-50 cells treated with the mixture of dot–Abmicrotubule (green) and dot–Abnucleus (gray) materials was evaluated under TPE at an excitation wavelength of 980 nm, power density of 160.0 nJ pixel−1, and depth of 260 μm. Regardless of whether laser exposure was applied (Fig. 7b and c), the KB-50 cells not treated with the mixture retained their integral morphology; for most of the KB-50 cells treated with the mixture but not subjected to photoexcitation, a clear image of cells and organelle locations could be obtained because of the successful counterstaining and conjugation of Abmicrotubule and Abnucleus (Fig. 7d). By contrast, the generation of ROS by the mixture may have led to oxidation and the deterioration of the surrounding biological substrates, resulting in distorted cells, atrophy, and severe morphological damage, which were captured by the TPI process (Fig. 7e). This suggests that amino-N-GQD12.3–Abnucleus had a favorable PDT effect on the cells and that the dots desorbed from the microtubules and nuclei compared with the cell alone (Fig. 7e); this implies that the dots had a disruptive effect on the cellular environment, causing the dots to detach from the organelles. Conversely, despite the unfavorable uptake efficiency of amino-N-GQD5.0 or amino-N-GQD12.3 (Fig. S19) for lacking of antibody conjugations, KB-50 cells treated with the mixture still exhibited a relatively distinguishable morphology (Fig. 7f). In addition, in the two-photon autofluorescence (TPAF) imaging of emissions from intrinsic fluorophores (gray-level image) of the untreated cells (unlabeled cells), making observations at the same depth was difficult, which would not have confounded the TPI results (Fig. 7g) unless a higher energy level of approximately 2274 nJ pixel−1 (with 20,000 scans, corresponding to a total effective exposure time of 136 s under excitation at a wavelength of 980 nm) was used (Fig. 7h). Hence, the TPI results could not have been confounded. In this study, signals from either the labeled (Fig. 7i) or the unlabeled cells (Fig. 7j) could not be detected, signifying that confocal laser scanning microscopy (one-photon excitation) may not be an effective method for detecting changes in cells treated with amino-N-GQDs. In addition, the surviving viabilities of KB-50 cells were indicated by fluorescence and quantification (Fig. S20) (Kuo et al., 2020). Regardless of whether laser exposure was applied, the cells showed nearly no damage, as evidenced by the green fluorescence, indicating live cells (Figs. S20a and b), as well as the panel of the KB-50 cells treated with the mixture-Abs but not subjected to photoexcitation (Fig. S20c); the dead cells from the mixture-Abs treatments and laser exposure were distinguishable to a particular degree, as indicated by the red fluorescence (Fig. S20d); for lacking of antibody conjugations, cells treated with the mixture still exhibited a relatively discernible morphology (Fig. S20e). The viability of cells was quantified for additional tests, which showed nearly all materials-treated cells to be dead after treatment (Fig. S20f). Additionally, capturing high-quality images at depths greater than 260 μm by using a laser optical system can be challenging owing to spherical aberrations caused by several factors such as a refractive index mismatch, poor detection efficiency, and objective lens settings (Richardson and Lichtman, 2015). Aberrations can result in greatly reduced image quality, making it difficult to detect certain types of TPL emissions, such as TPI. The maximum z–depth for detecting TPL when using a laser optical system is limited to 260 μm due to the optimization of the detection efficiency and set objective for examining amino-N-GQDs as two-photon contrast probes. Consequently, TPI was not detected at a depth of 280 μm for the mixture (Fig. S21).

The presence of N dopants and amino groups on the surface of GQDs can affect not only radiative electron–hole pair recombination but also the electronic properties of the GQDs. In particular, the presence of edge amine groups on GQDs can increase the maximum occupied molecular orbital energy of the graphene flakes. The maximum occupied molecular orbital energy is an essential property that influences the electronic and optical properties of GQDs (Tetsuka et al., 2012). In GQD-based materials, the maximum occupied molecular orbital energy determines the energy required to excite an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, and this energy is related to the materials' band-gap energy. The presence of edge amine groups on GQDs can increase the maximum occupied molecular orbital energy by introducing additional N atoms to the graphene lattice, thus modifying the electronic structure of the materials. This modification can lead to a narrower band gap and increased absorption from the UV to the NIR-I and NIR-II regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. Therefore, the presence of N dopants and amino groups on the surface of GQDs can have strong impacts on the dots’ electronic and optical properties. According to the current XPS analysis, the presence of C–O, C=O, O=C–O, and amide–N groups in the smallest GQDs induced nonradiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs, which prevents intrinsic-state emission (Jiang et al., 2019; Yoon et al., 2016). Furthermore, the QY increased with the particle size of the prepared materials. This could have been due to a decrease in the surface-to-volume ratio of the materials, which typically results in a smaller contribution of surface defects to nonradiative recombination. An increase in particle size typically engenders a decrease in the density of localized energy states within the band gap, reducing the efficiency of nonradiative recombination and increasing the probability of intrinsic-state emission. Additionally, edge sites are the sites on the edges of GQDs where C atoms are exposed. As the number of edge sites increases, the number of nitrogen-containing groups—such as C–N, amino–N, pyrrolic–N, and amide–N groups—also increases. These N-containing groups are responsible for the cancer elimination properties of the amino-N-GQDs prepared in this study. The size of amino-N-GQDs affects the elimination ability and generation of ROS. When amino-N-GQDs are larger, their elimination ability is higher. This is because the larger size enables more extensive interactions with bacterial cells. The larger size also leads to the generation of a higher quantity of ROS, which is crucial in PDT. Chemical modifications can strongly affect the electronic properties of GQDs. Functionalization with primary amine molecules results in strong electron donation, which can influence the electronic structure of GQDs (Wang et al., 2009). Furthermore, singlet and triplet states are different energy states that molecules can occupy. Singlet states involve two electrons with opposite spins, whereas triplet states involve two electrons with the same spin. Intersystem crossing is the process in which a molecule transitions from a singlet state to a triplet state (or vice versa) because the spin of its electrons is altered. The singlet–triplet splitting of amino-N-GQDs causes them to transition from a singlet state to a triplet state, resulting in a high yield of triplet states. This process is efficient and can compete with internal conversion, a process in which a molecule transitions between multiple identical states with the same energy. The competition between singlet–triplet splitting and internal conversion leads to the creation of ROS, which can be used in PDT to eliminate cancer cells (Moon et al., 2021; Park et al., 2020). In summary, the sorted amino-N-GQD-based materials produced in this study were discovered to have superior properties to the sorted dots (i.e., undoped GQDs, nitrogen-doped GQDs, amino-functionalized GQDs) under TPE, which is a characteristic crucial to their potential use in various applications such as bioimaging and sensing. The long-term stability of the sorted amino-N-GQDs should be further investigated under various conditions to fully understand their potential for practical applications.

This study revealed that the chemical modification of sorted GQD-based materials through N doping and amino group functionalization (to produce amino-N-GQDs) effectively increased the efficiency of charge transfer in sorted GQDs. After undergoing TPE, the as-prepared sorted GQD-based materials exhibited EWI-TPL from the UV to the NIR-I and NIR-II regions and exhibited favorable electrochemical and photochemical activities. The favorable two-photon properties of the sorted GQD-based materials in the NIR region can be attributed to their large lateral size, as achieved through sorting, and their N functionalities, which induce radiative recombination of localized electron–hole pairs. The N dopants and amino groups on the surface of the sorted GQDs were found to confer the dots with promising qualities that render them suitable for use as two-photon contrast probes and PSs in PDT; that is, they can generate a considerable number of ROS and can achieve high photoinactivation efficacy. The findings of this study provide insights into the mechanisms underlying the photodynamic properties of functionalized sorted dots and their favorable properties for TPI to track and localize analytes, thereby informing the development of more effective biomedical applications in dual-modality two-photon PDT and imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibody (Ab) conjugation and zeta potential (Ning et al., 2008; Rizvi et al., 2017)
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance of a quantity of antibody was recorded via UV–vis spectroscopy (Absorbance: approximately from 250 to 300 nm; Fig. S22). According to the manufacturer's instructions (Conjugation Kit, abcam, Cambridge, Cambridge, UK; Modification kit, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA), the materials were reacted with the same quantity antibody overnight in the dark (4 °C) and then centrifuged (approximately 847,200 RCF, >10 min) to remove excess Ab; the material-Ab(aq) was then prepared. Conversely, the supernatant was retained and its absorbance measured. The difference between the absorbance of the collected supernatant and the original antibody was estimated. Consequentially, the quantity of the Ab conjugated on the materials was calculated by Lambert-Beer's law. In the working solution of 1 × PBS buffer, approximately 26.2 μg of AbEGFR (Cambridge, UK) conjugated on 100 μg of GQD0.7. This implies that the conjugation efficiency of 1 × PBS buffer was approximately 26.2%, and it was 26.5%, 26.7%, 26.3%, 26.5%, 26.9%, 26.4%, 26.7%, 26.9%, 26.4%, 26.8% and 27.0% for GQD4.8-AbEGFR, GQD12.2-AbEGFR, N-GQD0.8-AbEGFR, N-GQD4.9-AbEGFR, N-GQD12.2-AbEGFR, amino-GQD0.8-AbEGFR, amino-GQD5.0-AbEGFR, amino-GQD12.3-AbEGFR, amino-N-GQD0.9-AbEGFR, amino-N-GQD5.0-AbEGFR, and amino-N-GQD12.3-AbEGFR, respectively. On the other hand, the zeta potential of the sorted material was also recorded by the spectrometer (Malvern Nano-ZS90, Worcestershire, West Midlands, UK). The materials' values of zeta potential were as follows: 12.4 mV, 12.9 mV, 13.1 mV, 12.5 mV, 12.7 mV, 13.3 mV, 12.7 mV, 13.0 mV, 13.2 mV, 12.6 mV, 13.1 mV and 13.3 mV for GQD4.8-AbEGFR, GQD12.2-AbEGFR, N-GQD0.8-AbEGFR, N-GQD4.9-AbEGFR, N-GQD12.2-AbEGFR, amino-GQD0.8-AbEGFR, amino-GQD5.0-AbEGFR, amino-GQD12.3-AbEGFR, amino-N-GQD0.9-AbEGFR, amino-N-GQD5.0-AbEGFR, and amino-N-GQD12.3-AbEGFR, respectively, in the working solution of 1 × PBS buffer; the conjugation efficiency of anti-microtubule antibody (Abmicrotubule; Cambridge, UK) was approximately 32.3% for amino-N-GQD5.0 and its value of zeta potential was 16.8 mV in the working solution of 1 × PBS buffer; the conjugation efficiency of anti-nucleus and mitotic cell antigen antibody (Abnucleus; Cambridge, UK) was approximately 29.1% for amino-N-GQD12.3 and its value of zeta potential was 14.4 mV in the working solution of 1 × PBS buffer [Cell type: DTS1060C; Measurement duration: 30 number of runs, 20 run duration (sec); delivered dose a solution of material: 4 mg mL−1].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular weight of sorted GQD-based materials
ce:section.ce:para: The theoretical diameter of a benzene is 0.243 nm with the molecular weight of 72 (ignore the H atoms). According to Fig. 3 and Fig. S1, the mean lateral size of sorted GQD-based materials was approximately 0.7 ± 0.1 nm (GQD0.7), 4.8 ± 0.2 nm (GQD4.8), 12.2 ± 0.2 nm (GQD12.2), 0.8 ± 0.1 nm (N-GQD0.8), 4.9 ± 0.2 nm (N-GQD4.9), 12.2 ± 0.3 nm (N-GQD12.2), 0.8 ± 0.1 (amino-GQD0.8), 5.0 ± 0.3 nm (amino-GQD5.0), 12.3 ± 0.2 nm (amino-GQD12.3), 0.9 ± 0.1 nm (amino-N-GQD0.9), 5.0 ± 0.2 nm (amino-N-GQD5.0), and 12.3 ± 0.4 nm (amino-N-GQD12.3), respectively. For GQD0.7, GQD4.8 and GQD12.2, assuming there is no leakage from a layer of material and ignoring the exposed functional groups, and then the benzene number and molecular weight are expected to be approximately 7 and 288 g mol−1, 487 and 7200 g mol−1, 3751 and 48,672 g mol−1 (Table 1 ), respectively. According to the assumption mentioned above and the atomic ratios and bonding compositions, the benzene number and molecular weight of N-GQD0.8, N-GQD4.9 and N-GQD12.2 are expected to be approximately 7 and 290 g mol−1, 610 and 8732 g mol−1, 3751 and 48,722 g mol−1, respectively; the benzene number and molecular weight of amino-GQD0.8, amino-GQD5.0 and amino-GQD12.3 are expected to be approximately 7 and 290 g mol−1, 610 and 8732 g mol−1, 3751 and 48,722 g mol−1, respectively; the benzene number and molecular weight of amino-N-GQD0.9, amino-N-GQD5.0 and amino-N-GQD12.3 are expected to be approximately 7 and 292 g mol−1, 610 and 8734 g mol−1, 3751 and 48,724 g mol−1, respectively. The following measurement for the cross section of TPE was available using the estimated molecular weights.
All Materials and methods for this article can be found in the Supplementary Information.

Wen-Shuo Kuo: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, and, Validation. Chia-Yuan Chang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, and, Validation.
